data.title: Apple says more personalized version of Siri is delayed
data.description:
• Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) said the updated, more personalized version of its digital voice assistant Siri has been delayed to 2026.
  • "It's going to take us longer than we thought to deliver on these features and we anticipated rolling them out in the coming year," the iPhone maker said in a statement obtained by Reuters.
  • No reason was given for the delay, but Seeking Alpha has previously reported that it has run into some engineering issues with the next version of its Siri assistant. The new Siri is reported to utilize more advanced large language models and could potentially allow for back-and-forth conversations and handle requests in a quicker manner.
  • Cupertino, Calif.-based Apple announced that a new version of its Siri assistant would be coming at its 2024 developer conference.
  • Apple shares rose 1.5% in midday trading on Friday.

data.title: Lumine Group reports Q4 results
data.description:
• Lumine Group press release (OTCPK:LMGIF): Q4 Revenue grew 31% to $187.1 million compared to $143.1 million in the same quarter prior year (including -9% organic growth after adjusting for foreign exchange impacts)
  • The Company generated net income of $29.4 million during the quarter, from net loss of $1,506.3 million in the same quarter prior year.

data.title: CDC reports 58 new measles cases as nationwide count surpasses 200
data.description:
• A weekly update from the CDC on the number of reported measles cases states that as of March 6, there are 222, 58 more than the prior week.
  • Most of those cases -- 99 (45%) -- are in individuals between 5 and 19 years old. Another 76 (34%) cases are in youngsters below the age of 5.
  • Of the 222 cases, 94% are either unvaccinated or vaccination status unknown.
  • A total of 38 cases (17%) are patients who are hospitalized.
  • Late Thursday, the New Mexico Department of Health disclosed a death linked to measles. That patient was unvaccinated.
  • Merck (NYSE:MRK) and GSK (NYSE:GSK) market the two MMR vaccines available in the U.S.

data.title: Atrium Mortgage Investment GAAP EPS of C$1.05, revenue of C$97.26M
data.description:
• Atrium Mortgage Investment press release (OTCPK:AMIVF): FY GAAP EPS of C$1.05.
  • Revenue of C$97.26M (-1.3% Y/Y).

data.title: Trump says he may impose tariffs on Canadian lumber, dairy products today
data.description:
President Donald Trump said Friday that he may impose reciprocal tariffs on imported labor and dairy products from Canada, possibly almost immediately. 

“They’ll be met with the exact same tariff unless they drop it, and that’s what reciprocal means,” Trump said in the Oval Office. “We may do it as early as today, or we’ll wait till Monday or Tuesday, but that’s what we’re going to do. We’re going to do, we’re going to charge.”

Trump on Thursday delayed tariffs on Canadian and Mexican imports that are covered by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement trade pact that he negotiated with the neighbors during his first term. 

“They make it impossible for us to sell lumber or dairy products into Canada,” Trump said.

Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau’s office did not immediately respond to a request for comment by Seeking Alpha.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: White House policies' net effect is what matters, Federal Reserve's Jerome Powell says
data.description:
The new White House administration is making major changes in trade, immigration, fiscal policies, and deregulation. "It's the net effect of these policies" that matters, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said on Friday at the University of Chicago Booth School of Business.

It wouldn't be appropriate for the Fed to react to a one-time price spike from tariffs. "What really matter is long-term inflation expectations," he explained.

Overall, though, the U.S. economy remains healthy, he noted. The labor market is solid and inflation has been moving toward the central bank's 2% goal, he said.

"Many indicators show that the labor market is solid and broadly in balance," he said in prepared remarks.

The unemployment rate remains low near 4%. The labor market is not a source of wage inflation, Powell added.

In discussing the Fed's review of its monetary policy framework, Powell said he doesn't think the central bank needs to redefine price stability, which it defines as 2% inflation.

Breaking news... check back for updates.

data.title: J.P. Morgan defends Carvana
data.description:
The topic of Carvana’s (NYSE:CVNA) valuation has been a hot topic among investors recently, as the stock’s meteoric rise of +140% year-over-year is in stark comparison to much more modest – if any – gains for peers like CarMax (KMX), AutoNation (AN), Sonic Automotive (SAH), and CarGurus (CARG).

Since reporting Q4 results, the share price has retreated by almost 40% and breached support at the 200-day moving average for the first time in almost a year, as the stock’s nosebleed level encouraged some profit-taking by risk-adverse investors.

Among those remaining a strong advocate of Carvana’s business model is J.P. Morgan’s auto analyst Rajat Gupta who raised his price target to $365, a level that is nearly twice where the stock closed on Thursday. Gupta bases this very bullish outlook on his confidence that Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) can sustain its recent growth trajectory and improve its unit economics.

Supporting Gupta’s Overweight rating for Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) is its solid momentum across all operations, including finance. While rising delinquencies could hamper its relationship with Ally Financial (ALLY), Gupta sees the company’s loss performance better than peers on both prime and sub-prime, amplified by an increasing appetite for Carvana’s (CVNA) loans from both asset-backed and third-party lenders.

On the economic/tariff front, Carvana (CVNA) “is in a far stronger position, operationally and financially, than in 2022 to weather any economic shocks.” In addition, a “robust tax refund season” is likely to fuel increased traffic and provide room for “significant” upward revisions to current Q1 and 2025 estimates.

Carvana (CVNA) has also refined its vertical integration with expansion into the commercial marketplace, and new cars/off-lease/CPO vehicles through the acquisition of a Stellantis (STLA) franchise, all of which Gupta believes brings a much higher degree of efficiency and leverage with the potential for further gains in EBITDA/unit despite already at less than 2x their largest competitor. 

“With every quarter of progress on key KPIs and adjacencies, we believe Carvana’s moat is likely to widen, and the company is still in the early stages of reaping benefits of a tightly-knit vertically integrated infrastructure,” Gupta says, adding that the investments in its network, including the ADESA physical auction business are proving to be long-term competitive advantages that are hard to replicate.

data.title: Firms say US must work with allies to counter China's goal of semiconductor dominance
data.description:
U.S. semiconductor organizations and companies say the U.S. Trade Commission must look beyond tariffs, and also embrace working with global allies, to stave off China's attempts to dominate the industry.

The U.S. International Trade Commission plans to hold a public hearing on March 11 related to China's policies and practices to dominate the global semiconductor industry.

It will feature presentations by semiconductor firms such as Wolfspeed (NYSE:WOLF) and ACM Research (NASDAQ:ACMR) as well as industry groups and political coalitions and organizations.

"In semiconductors, as in other advanced technologies, China has pursued a wide range of policies and practices that seek to frustrate access for foreign products and companies to its domestic market, while offering an array of subsidies, discriminatory standards, preferential government procurement policies, local content requirements, and other measures intended to boost local producers and design-in domestically produced semiconductor chips into downstream products for sale in China's domestic market as well as for products exported to the rest of the world," the Semiconductor Industry Association said in a public comment filed ahead of the hearing.

The SIA, which includes charter members such as Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), AMD (NASDAQ:AMD), Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO), also pointed out that in December 2024 China "imposed an export ban of gallium, germanium, and antimony, critical minerals used in semiconductor manufacturing to the United States."

The SIA said solutions to these issues are best made through coordinated, multi-country solutions.

"We strongly urge USTR and other U.S. agencies to continue collaboration with G7 countries and other trusted partners and allies towards coordinated, multi-country solutions to the challenges outlined in this submission – solutions that maximize impact and minimize potential free-riding and backfilling," the association added.

The USTR needs to consider tariff and non-tariff actions to counter China, which includes working with allies, according to a public comment submitted by the North Carolina-based Wolfspeed.

"Accordingly, since China's silicon carbide substrate market share is too large to be affected by U.S. actions alone, the United States should call on its allies to act as well," the company noted. "Wolfspeed respectfully submits that USTR should coordinate with its allies to develop global counter initiatives. It is time to address the distortionary effects of PRC state action on the semiconductor global supply chain."

ACM Research said the U.S. needs to expand its manufacturing capabilities.

"To support that goal, we urge the Administration to protect the global supply chains on which the U.S. semiconductor manufacturers rely," ACM said. "In particular, the equipment and machinery provided by ACMR are critical to the domestic manufacturers' operations and to expand semiconductor manufacturing in the United States."

"To the extent that the current investigation results in the imposition of additional tariffs, machinery and equipment used for domestic manufacturing should be exempted from any such tariffs," they added.

data.title: 10 oversold stocks that are outperforming the S&P 500
data.description:
Wall Street faces renewed challenges this Friday, with volatility and uncertainty casting a shadow over market sentiment after the U.S. nonfarm payrolls data came in below expectations. Adding to the pressure are ongoing tariff discussions, pushing stocks lower across the board. Yet, amidst this turbulence, a select group of stocks—classified as oversold according to the Relative Strength Index (RSI)—are managing to still outperform the S&P 500 (SP500).

As of 2025, the S&P 500 is down 3.8%, while stocks in oversold territory (RSI readings of 30 or below) have shown some levels of resilience. 

Below, Seeking Alpha highlighted the top ten best-performing oversold stocks year-to-date, offering a glimmer of opportunity in an otherwise challenging market. List is ranked by YTD performance. 

No. 10: Hancock Whitney Corporation (HWC), -3% YTD.

No. 9: Accenture plc (CAN), -2.8% YTD.

No. 8: Truist Financial Corporation (TFC), -2.6% YTD.

No. 7: Suncor Energy (SU), -2.6% YTD.

No. 6: MidCap Financial Investment Corporation (MFIC), -2.3% YTD.

No. 5: Golub Capital BDC (GBDC), -2.3% YTD.

No. 4: Citigroup (C), +0.2% YTD.

No. 3: Seagate Technology Holdings (STX), +0.4% YTD.

No. 2: TD SYNNEX Corporation (SNX), +10.2% YTD. 

No. 1: LATAM Airlines Group (LTM), +17.6% YTD. 

Moreover, see what each stocks RSI reading looks like: HWC 28, ACN 28, TFC 29, SU 29, MFIC 27, GBDC 28, C 30, STX 27, SNX 29, and LTM 18.

data.title: Nvidia loses $1T market cap since hitting record high earlier this year
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) has lost about $1T in market cap since hitting a record high earlier this year and has firmly fallen out of the coveted $3T club.

The U.S. tech giant has seen its stock slip more than 20% since the start of the year, as investors express concerns about the U.S.-China tech tussle and fears of cheaper AI models from the likes of Chinese AI startup DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) entering the market. 

January 27 saw around $589B erased from Nvidia's market cap after DeepSeek unveiled its AI model R1. The AI lab also detailed in a paper how large language models could be built on tight budgets and improved without human supervision, though some have expressed skepticism about DeepSeek's actual budget.

The sell-off has accelerated in recent days, as shares of the Jensen Huang-led company have tumbled nearly 10% as President Trump has ratcheted up tariff-related rhetoric on China, Canada and Mexico. There's also concern that companies in China are using workarounds to get the latest offerings from Nvidia, despite export control curbs placed on Nvidia's most advanced offerings.

In the last five days, Nvidia's stock has fallen about 10%. As of March 7, the company's market cap is about $2.7T.

Nvidia was the third U.S. company to reach a market value of $3T, joining Apple (AAPL) and software giant Microsoft (MSFT). Only Apple remains.
